source: Rocky Linux Admin Guide, ch. 17 Conclusions - permission tips

questions.q: Why is 'chmod -R 644 /target' on a directory tree a mistake?
options: It strips the execute bit from every subdirectory, so no one can cd into them anymore
questions.why: Directories need x to be entered; 644 removes it recursively, locking everyone out of the subdirectories.

questions.q: Safely set mode 644 on only the FILES under /target (not the directories), using find with -exec.
questions.why: -type f targets only files; the safer one-shot alternative is 'chmod -R u=rwX,go=rX /target' where capital X sets x only on directories.

questions.q: What is the effect of 'chmod -R u=rwX,go=rX /target' (note the capital X)?
options: The capital X sets the execute bit only on directories (and files that already had it), not on plain files
questions.why: Capital X applies x only to directories and already-executable files, so it fixes a tree in one command without making every file executable.

questions.q: Set the setgid bit on the directory /share so new files inherit the directory's group.
questions.why: setgid on a directory forces group inheritance - the essential trick for team shares.

questions.q: What is the point of giving a group a password with gpasswd?
options: Someone who knows it can newgrp into that group without being a member; gpasswd -A also names group administrators
questions.why: A group password lets a non-member newgrp into the group; gpasswd -A designates admins who can add/remove members without root.

questions.q: Make /etc/resolv.conf immutable so nothing (even a service on reboot) can rewrite it.
questions.why: chattr +i blocks all changes until you run chattr -i - a safeguard against an accidental rm or a service that keeps rewriting the file.
